hungarian_parliament.description: Neo-Gothic riverside parliament with a tall central dome and long symmetrical wings—Hungary’s largest building and a postcard symbol of Budapest.
hungarian_parliament.history: Built from the late 19th century after the 1867 Compromise; designed by Imre Steindl. Heavily damaged in World War II and restored in stages.
hungarian_parliament.significance: Seat of the National Assembly and a national emblem of Hungarian statehood.
facts: Guided visits cover the dome hall and ceremonial stairs when sessions allow.
The building faces the Danube across from Buda Castle and the Chain Bridge.

chain_bridge.address: Connects Széchenyi Square (Buda) and Clark Ádám Square (Pest)
chain_bridge.description: The first permanent bridge linking Buda and Pest, with stone towers and suspension chains—still a main pedestrian and tram crossing.
chain_bridge.history: Opened in 1849; named after Count István Széchenyi. Rebuilt after World War II using original design principles.
chain_bridge.significance: Urban link that literally unified the two cities long before their official merger.
facts: Lion sculptures guard the abutments at each end.
Night lighting makes it one of the most photographed spans in Europe.

fishermans_bastion.address: Hess András Square, Buda Castle district
fishermans_bastion.description: Fairytale terraces and turrets above the Danube with views toward Parliament—built as a lookout, not a fortress.
fishermans_bastion.history: Completed around 1902 to designs by Frigyes Schulek near Matthias Church; partly fee-charging for upper terraces.
fishermans_bastion.significance: Romantic panorama point and wedding-photo favourite above the river bend.
facts: Named after the fishermen’s guild once tasked with defending this stretch of wall.
Seven towers evoke the seven Magyar chieftains of legend.

matthias_church.description: Colourful tiled roof and mixed Gothic lines: coronations and royal weddings once took place under its vaults.
matthias_church.history: Core from the 13th–15th centuries with later Baroque and 19th-century Neo-Gothic restoration by Frigyes Schulek.
matthias_church.significance: Buda’s main church for centuries and a visual anchor next to the Fisherman’s Bastion.
facts: Franz Joseph and Elizabeth were crowned king and queen of Hungary here in 1867.
Interior frescoes and the organ draw concert audiences.

st_stephens_basilica.description: Neoclassical basilica with a climbable dome; houses the reliquary of St. Stephen, Hungary’s first king.
st_stephens_basilica.history: Construction spanned the 19th century after an earlier church collapse; dome completed with engineering lessons from the saga.
st_stephens_basilica.significance: Co-cathedral of Esztergom–Budapest and a major venue for state and liturgical events.
facts: The panorama terrace rewards the stair or lift climb.
The Holy Right (hand relic) is displayed on notable feast days.

szechenyi_bath.description: Yellow Neo-Baroque bath complex with outdoor pools steamy in winter—social Budapest at its most relaxed.
szechenyi_bath.history: Opened in 1913; water is supplied from the Széchenyi thermal spring system under the park.
szechenyi_bath.significance: One of Europe’s largest spa complexes and a symbol of Budapest’s bath culture.
facts: Chess players at outdoor pools are a familiar winter sight.
Combine with Heroes’ Square and Vajdahunyad Castle on foot.

heroes_square.address: End of Andrássy Avenue, City Park entrance
heroes_square.description: Monumental square with the Millennium Memorial, colonnades of historic figures, and two major art museums on either side.
heroes_square.history: Laid out for Hungary’s 1896 millennium celebrations; later adapted as a site for mass gatherings and national holidays.
heroes_square.significance: Hungarian history in stone—mythic chieftains, kings, and independence themes in one vista.
facts: The Museum of Fine Arts and the Hall of Art frame the square.
Ice skating and the zoo lie deeper in the park behind.

buda_castle.description: Royal palace complex housing the Hungarian National Gallery, the Budapest History Museum, and National Széchényi Library.
buda_castle.history: Royal seat from the 13th century onward; repeatedly destroyed and rebuilt—today’s shell largely mid–20th century.
buda_castle.significance: UNESCO-listed Buda Castle Quarter core with ramparts, churches, and cobbled streets.
facts: Funicular from Clark Ádám Square saves the steep climb.
Courtyards and terraces give wide Pest-side panoramas.

great_market_hall.description: Iron-framed hall with a colourful Zsolnay roof: paprika, lángos, and souvenirs on the ground, more food upstairs.
great_market_hall.history: Opened in 1897; designed by Samu Pecz as a modern wholesale market near the river and tram termini.
great_market_hall.significance: Working market and tourist ritual in one noisy, fragrant building.
facts: Try the gallery level for simple Hungarian dishes.
Liberty Bridge is steps away for a riverside walk.

hungarian_state_opera.description: Renaissance Revival opera house with rich auditorium gilding—home to opera and ballet after a major recent restoration.
hungarian_state_opera.history: Opened in 1884 to Miklós Ybl’s design; acoustics were praised from the first season.
hungarian_state_opera.significance: One of Europe’s great lyric theatres and a UNESCO-listed stretch of Andrássy Avenue.
facts: Guided tours run when rehearsals allow.
The avenue is lined with cafés toward Heroes’ Square.

margaret_island.address: Danube between Margaret Bridge and Árpád Bridge
margaret_island.description: Green kilometre-long island: promenades, ruins, a small zoo, pools, and summer musical fountain shows.
margaret_island.history: Monasteries once stood here; today it is a public park with sports facilities and spas.
margaret_island.significance: Local lung between Buda and Pest—runners, families, and festival crowds share the paths.
facts: Rent bikes or electric carts to circle the island quickly.
Medieval Dominican convent ruins remain near the centre.

gellert_hill_view.description: Limestone hill above the Danube: Citadel, Liberty Statue, and lookout paths over bridges and rooftops.
gellert_hill_view.history: Fortifications from the 19th century; the giant female Liberty figure dates to Soviet-era commemoration, reinterpreted today.
gellert_hill_view.significance: Classic sunset viewpoint and a geological backdrop to the Gellért Baths below.
facts: The Cave Church is carved into the hillside near the base.
Wear sturdy shoes; some paths are steep and exposed.

shoes_danube_memorial.description: Sixty pairs of cast iron shoes recall Jews shot into the river by Arrow Cross militia in 1944–45.
shoes_danube_memorial.history: Created by Gyula Pauer and Can Togay (2005) as a quiet riverside memorial.
shoes_danube_memorial.significance: One of Budapest’s most moving Holocaust sites—flowers and candles appear daily.
facts: Visit at dusk when the Parliament lights reflect on the water.
Keep a respectful distance; not a selfie backdrop.

dohany_street_synagogue.description: Moorish Revival synagogue—largest in Europe by seating—with museum and cemetery garden in the block.
dohany_street_synagogue.history: Built 1854–1859; the ghetto wall during the siege of Budapest ran partly through the complex.
dohany_street_synagogue.significance: Centre of Neolog Judaism in Hungary and a key site on Jewish heritage walks.
facts: The Raoul Wallenberg Holocaust Memorial Park adjoins the site.
Guided visits explain the organ and women’s gallery.

vajdahunyad_castle.description: Romantic composite castle copying Transylvanian and Romanesque motifs—originally temporary for 1896, rebuilt in stone.
vajdahunyad_castle.history: Designed by Ignác Alpár as a millennium exhibition pavilion; today it houses the Hungarian Agricultural Museum.
vajdahunyad_castle.significance: Fairytale silhouette on the boating lake minutes from Széchenyi Bath.
facts: Rowboats and ice skating (seasonal) surround the building.
Courtyard concerts enliven summer evenings.

liberty_bridge.address: Connects Fővám Square (Pest) and Gellért Square (Buda)
liberty_bridge.description: Green Art Nouveau truss bridge with a central pedestrian strip—popular at sunset and during summer festivals.
liberty_bridge.history: Opened in 1896 as Franz Joseph Bridge; renamed after 1945. Trams share the deck with walkers.
liberty_bridge.significance: Visual neighbour to the Great Market Hall and Gellért Hotel.
facts: The Turul bird statue tops a Buda-side pillar.
Occasionally closed to traffic for public gatherings.

andrassy_avenue.address: Andrássy út, linking Deák Square to Heroes’ Square
andrassy_avenue.description: Grand boulevard lined with mansions, embassies, Opera, and House of Terror museum—UNESCO buffer around the Millennium Metro below.
andrassy_avenue.history: Laid out from 1872 as a representative axis for the growing capital.
facts: Metro M1 is continental Europe’s oldest underground line (1896).
Cafés on side streets offer quieter breaks than the main road.

new_york_cafe.description: Gilded coffee palace—paintings, stucco, and chandeliers in a revived fin-de-siècle interior.
new_york_cafe.history: Opened in 1894; fell into decay under communism, then lavishly restored as a hotel café.
new_york_cafe.significance: Tourist-priced but unmatched for Belle Époque atmosphere.
facts: Reservations help at peak hours.
Live music on many evenings.

rudas_baths.description: Ottoman-era dome and octagonal pool under pencil columns, plus newer rooftop tubs with Danube views.
rudas_baths.history: Turkish bath from the 16th century, expanded across centuries; gendered days still apply in some thermal areas.
rudas_baths.significance: Direct experience of Budapest’s Turkish layer beneath the modern spa industry.
facts: Check the calendar for men-only, women-only, or mixed days.
Combine with Gellért Bath across the river if time allows.

museum_of_fine_arts.description: Neoclassical temple of art facing the Hall of Art—Old Masters, Egyptian antiquities, and a strong Spanish collection.
museum_of_fine_arts.history: Opened in 1906; long renovation cycles refreshed galleries and climate systems.
museum_of_fine_arts.significance: Hungary’s premier encyclopaedic fine arts museum.
facts: Ticket often pairs with temporary blockbusters—check schedules.
The building balances the square’s monument symmetrically.

church_1_budapest.description: A text-only church entry added for itinerary balance and slower walking routes.
facts: Text-only entry to keep PDF builds stable.
Can be replaced with a sourced church later.
church_1_budapest.history: Added as a structured placeholder with substantive text (no image).
church_1_budapest.significance: Helps diversify the guide with more sacred architecture stops.
